title: Sourcegraph license key tokens should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: Sourcegraph license key detected in source code. Sourcegraph license keys are cryptographically signed tokens that activate and validate enterprise subscriptions for self-hosted Sourcegraph instances, controlling access to premium features, user limits, and enterprise capabilities. These license keys grant comprehensive entitlements including: activating enterprise-level features and functionality, controlling maximum user count and concurrent sessions, enabling code intelligence and precise code navigation features, activating batch changes and code campaign capabilities, enabling code insights and analytics dashboards, controlling search performance and indexing features, activating enterprise authentication providers (SSO/SAML/LDAP), enabling audit logging and compliance reporting, controlling API rate limits and quotas, activating browser extensions and IDE integrations, enabling webhooks and external integrations, controlling repository access limits, activating code monitoring and alerting features, enabling saved searches and search contexts, controlling code host connections (GitHub/GitLab Enterprise), activating precise code intelligence indexers, enabling notebooks and search notebooks, controlling extensions and extension registry access, activating site-level admin capabilities, and determining subscription expiration and renewal terms. Exposing Sourcegraph license keys enables attackers to: activate unauthorized Sourcegraph instances using your enterprise subscription, consume your licensed user seats preventing legitimate users from accessing the platform, deploy competing or malicious Sourcegraph instances with your enterprise features, bypass license limitations to access premium features without authorization, understand your enterprise subscription limits and deployment scale, identify which premium features your organization has licensed, potentially clone your instance configuration with full enterprise capabilities, deplete your support entitlements through fraudulent usage, cause license compliance violations triggering audit flags, force early license expiration through excessive activation attempts, gather intelligence about your enterprise Sourcegraph deployment architecture, access enterprise features intended only for your organization, create shadow IT instances that bypass security controls, potentially inject malicious configurations into cloned instances, compromise license renewal negotiations by exposing current entitlements, and escalate attacks by combining license access with other exposed credentials. This represents a critical intellectual property and subscription fraud risk requiring immediate action. Take immediate action: revoke the exposed license key immediately by contacting Sourcegraph support, request emergency license key rotation from your Sourcegraph account representative, generate new license key with updated activation restrictions, update all Sourcegraph instances with the new license key, conduct comprehensive audit of all Sourcegraph instance activations, review Sourcegraph access logs for unauthorized license usage, verify no unauthorized instances were activated using the exposed key, check for license seat consumption anomalies indicating fraudulent use, review all deployment configurations for additional exposed secrets, verify license activation IP addresses match your infrastructure, audit all infrastructure-as-code repositories for hardcoded license keys, implement principle of least privilege for license key access (restrict to deployment engineers only), store license keys in dedicated enterprise secret management systems with strict access controls, use configuration management tools with encryption for license deployment, never commit license keys to any repository including private infrastructure repos, implement comprehensive secret scanning across all repositories and configuration files, rotate license keys on defined schedule (annually at minimum or after personnel changes), require MFA for all accounts with license key access privileges, implement IP whitelisting for Sourcegraph instance activation where possible, monitor and alert on license activation attempts from unexpected locations, maintain detailed inventory of all authorized Sourcegraph instances with activation dates, implement break-glass procedures for emergency license key rotation, segregate license management from day-to-day operations access, regularly audit license usage against authorized instance inventory, educate deployment and infrastructure teams about license key security criticality, integrate license key detection in infrastructure-as-code and configuration scanning, implement approval workflows for new instance deployments requiring license activation, conduct regular security reviews of Sourcegraph deployment procedures and license handling, establish secure handoff procedures for license keys during deployment and maintenance, and coordinate with Sourcegraph support to implement license pinning or instance-specific restrictions where available.
